clusters.title: Clare Wright wins top prize at NSW Literary Awards for 'powerful' history book Näku Dhäruk The Bark Petitions
clusters.neutral_headline: Clare Wright wins top prize at NSW Literary Awards for 'powerful' history...
bullet_summary: Reported by ABC News Australia, Guardian Australia

items.title: Clare Wright wins top prize at NSW Literary Awards for 'powerful' history book Näku Dhäruk The Bark Petitions
items.standfirst: The 1963 Näku Dhäruk or Yirrkala bark petitions led to Australia's first land rights law. A new book about them just won two prizes at the NSW Literary Awards.
items.content: The 1963 Näku Dhäruk or Yirrkala bark petitions led to Australia's first land rights law. A new book about them just won two prizes at the NSW Literary Awards.

items.title: ‘A book that should be read by all Australians’: Clare Wright wins book of the year at the NSW Literary awards
items.standfirst: The historian won $50,000 for her nonfiction book Näku Dhäruk: The Bark Petitions, which judges praised as deeply researched, ‘highly original’ and ‘vividly alive’A “highly original” nonfiction by Melbourne historian Clare Wright, charting the creation of the Yirrkala Bark Petitions – a seminal moment in Australia’s history of land rights – has won book of the year at the NSW literary awards.The Petitions were landmark documents presented by Yolŋu elders to the Australian parliament in 1963 on painted bark frames, which sought government intervention after a portion of Arnhem Land Reserve was licensed to a French mining company. Though it didn’t halt mining on the land, the petitions led to the first land rights legislation in Australia, the Aboriginal Land Rights (Northern Territory) Act 1976. Continue reading...
items.content: The historian won $50,000 for her nonfiction book Näku Dhäruk: The Bark Petitions, which judges praised as deeply researched, ‘highly original’ and ‘vividly alive’A “highly original” nonfiction by Melbourne historian Clare Wright, charting the creation of the Yirrkala Bark Petitions – a seminal moment in Australia’s history of land rights – has won book of the year at the NSW literary awards.The Petitions were landmark documents presented by Yolŋu elders to the Australian parliament in 1963 on painted bark frames, which sought government intervention after a portion of Arnhem Land Reserve was licensed to a French mining company. Though it didn’t halt mining on the land, the petitions led to the first land rights legislation in Australia, the Aboriginal Land Rights (Northern Territory) Act 1976. Continue reading...

clusters.neutral_headline: Woman hit in crash that closed major Queensland highway

items.title: Woman hit in crash that closed major Queensland highway

items.content: The M1 was closed south of Brisbane after a horror crash involving 10 cars.

items.title: Woman critical after being hit by ute in horror Queensland crash
items.standfirst: T wo crashes involving 10 vehicles have shut a major highway in both directions.
items.content: A woman is fighting for life after being hit by a ute while on the side of the M1 motorway following a crash south of Brisbane, triggering a second multi-vehicle crash that closed the road.The woman was standing on the side of the Pacific Motorway in Logan after an initial crash between two cars when she was struck by the ute about 3.40pm.That collision then caused a massive pile-up on the motorway, with up to 10 cars believed to have been involved.READ MORE: Woman held at knifepoint in shocking early morning carjacking The woman was taken to hospital with critical injuries.The driver of the ute is speaking with police.The Pacific Motorway remained closed northbound after 7pm as police continued their investigations.Anyone who witnessed the incident or has dashcam footage is urged to contact police. items.title: Police running out of room to store illegal cigarettes they’ve seized amid Australia’s booming illicit trade
items.standfirst: Inquiry into black market hears cost of destroying vapes has also soared, with some requiring manual dismantlingFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastPolice are struggling to store a growing wave of illegal cigarettes and vapes seized from criminals, with secure facilities at capacity and the high cost of destroying illicit products becoming prohibitive.The Australian federal police (AFP) has been spending as much as $13 a kilogram for vapes to be destroyed, with some companies requiring the cartridge, battery and heating element to be manually dismantled. Continue reading...

items.title: Pianist Jayson Gillham says he never considered apologising for Gaza comments, trial hears
items.standfirst: Federal court case begins for classical pianist who alleges Melbourne Symphony Orchestra unlawfully discriminated due to his viewsFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastA classical pianist who alleges the Melbourne Symphony Orchestra unlawfully discriminated against him because of his views on Israeli forces killing Palestinian journalists says he did not consider apologising for making the statement at the centre of the case.Pianist Jayson Gillham is suing the MSO over a cancelled Melbourne concert he was contracted to perform on 15 August 2024, a cancellation which he claims was an attempt to silence him over his stance on the Gaza conflict. Continue reading...

items.title: Big tobacco is exploiting fears of the illicit market to unwind health gains, experts warn
items.standfirst: Cigarettes kill 66 Australians every day, health groups say, criticising MPs who heard evidence from Philip Morris in secretFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastBig tobacco is exploiting fears of the illicit market to unwind longstanding health policies, leading health campaigners have warned, amid a parliamentary inquiry which took secret evidence from the cigarette manufacturer Philip Morris.A coalition of 15 health organisations including the Cancer Council and the Heart Foundation, as well as health experts and researchers, have accused the industry of trying to reshape public debate to push for major cuts to government excise. Continue reading...
items.content: Cigarettes kill 66 Australians every day, health groups say, criticising MPs who heard evidence from Philip Morris in secretFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastBig tobacco is exploiting fears of the illicit market to unwind longstanding health policies, leading health campaigners have warned, amid a parliamentary inquiry which took secret evidence from the cigarette manufacturer Philip Morris.A coalition of 15 health organisations including the Cancer Council and the Heart Foundation, as well as health experts and researchers, have accused the industry of trying to reshape public debate to push for major cuts to government excise.
